content.text: A metric is only as useful as the question you can ask of it. Names that are ambiguous, inconsistent, or unbounded in cardinality create dashboards that contradict each other, alert rules that fire on the wrong conditions, and cost spikes in metric storage that nobody can explain.

are the organisational contracts that make every team's metrics queryable by every other team's tooling — and keep the cardinality budget from exploding as agentic AI systems add dozens of new dynamic dimensions (model names, agent IDs, tool names) to an already complex metric space.

Metric naming and tagging standards are a set of organisation-wide rules that govern three things: the structure of metric names (which namespaces, separators, unit suffixes, and aggregation-type suffixes are allowed), the set of dimensions (tags/labels) that must or may appear on each metric family, and the governance process for proposing, reviewing, and enforcing these rules across teams and over time.
In the context of agentic AI systems, this work is especially important because these systems introduce novel metric dimensions —

— that can easily become high-cardinality landmines if left uncontrolled. A model rollout that adds a new model version doubles the time series for every metric tagged with model. An agent that uses dynamic tool names (e.g., tool names that include user-provided strings) can explode the cardinality of any metric tagged with tool_name.
Without standards, the same underlying measurement gets named differently by each team. One team records
. When an on-call engineer needs to find the p99 latency for all LLM calls across the platform, they face three separate dashboard queries that may not agree. Shared naming eliminates this ambiguity.

The tagging dimension is equally critical. Prometheus-style label cardinality is multiplicative: a metric with four tags each having ten values produces a time series fan-out of up to 10,000 series. Agentic AI tags are particularly risky because model names, agent versions, and tool names change frequently. Without an explicit cardinality budget and governance process, a single un-reviewed tag can cause the metric storage cost to double overnight.
Cross-team dashboards work without translation: any engineer can query any team's metrics using the same naming pattern.
Alert rules can be written once and applied to any service that follows the standard, rather than being cloned and adapted per team.
Cardinality budgets enforced at CI time prevent runaway metric storage costs before they reach production.
Unit suffixes (e.g., _seconds, _bytes, _total) make metric types self-documenting and prevent unit confusion in dashboards (milliseconds vs seconds plotted on the same axis).
Required tag sets ensure that cost attribution, model comparisons, and environment isolation work consistently across all AI metrics.
Standards require governance overhead: a review process for new metrics and tag additions, a schema registry, and periodic audits of existing metrics for compliance.

Strict naming rules can feel like bureaucracy to teams that want to move fast. The friction is intentional, but it must be low enough that teams don't route around it by publishing undocumented metrics.
Retroactive standardisation of existing metrics requires either a breaking rename (which breaks dashboards) or a dual-publish period (which doubles costs), followed by a deprecation cycle.
Cardinality limits on tags like agent_id can prevent fine-grained per-agent debugging if the limit is set too low. Tuning the limit requires knowing the expected agent fleet size in advance.
The recommended pattern for agentic AI metrics is:
: The top-level organisational or product grouping. For AI-specific metrics:
: A snake_case noun or noun phrase describing what is being measured:
: The SI unit of the measurement:
. Never use _ms or _millis — standardise on seconds.
. This makes the instrument type discoverable from the name alone.
Tags (Prometheus labels / OTel attributes on metrics) are classified into three tiers based on their cardinality risk:
: Must be present on every metric in the family. Values come from a closed enum. Examples:
: May be present but require an explicit cardinality budget documented in the schema registry. Examples:
(from a known set of at most 50 tools),
(stable identifiers from a registry, budget capped at 1,000).
: Must never appear as a metric tag. Examples:
(free text). These belong in traces and logs, not metrics.
The diagram below shows the anatomy of a well-formed metric name and the tag classification for agentic AI systems, including the cardinality budget by tier.

attrs.alt: Metric naming anatomy showing namespace, subsystem, metric name, unit suffix, and aggregation type components with good and bad examples

content.text: When a new AI service or agent emits its first metric — naming it correctly from the start is far cheaper than renaming it later.
When adding a new tag to an existing metric — this is when cardinality review is critical, because a new tag multiplies the series count for the entire metric history.
When onboarding a new team to the platform — the naming standard should be part of their onboarding checklist, not discovered after their first metric breaks a dashboard.
When conducting a metric audit after a cardinality spike — the standard provides the reference for which tags were approved and what their budgets were.
Registering and Emitting Metrics with OTel SDK

from opentelemetry import metrics
from opentelemetry.sdk.metrics import MeterProvider
from opentelemetry.sdk.metrics.export import PeriodicExportingMetricReader
from opentelemetry.exporter.otlp.proto.grpc.metric_exporter import OTLPMetricExporter

# Bootstrap metric SDK
reader = PeriodicExportingMetricReader(OTLPMetricExporter(), export_interval_millis=60_000)
provider = MeterProvider(metric_readers=[reader])
metrics.set_meter_provider(provider)

meter = metrics.get_meter("ai.agent", "1.0.0")

# ai.agent.llm.input_tokens.counter
# Naming: namespace=ai, subsystem=agent.llm, metric=input_tokens, aggregation=counter
input_tokens_counter = meter.create_counter(
    name="ai.agent.llm.input_tokens",
    description="Total input/prompt tokens sent to LLM providers.",
    unit="{tokens}",   # dimensionless count — use {tokens} not a unit suffix here
)

# ai.agent.llm.request.duration_seconds.histogram
llm_duration_histogram = meter.create_histogram(
    name="ai.agent.llm.request.duration_seconds",
    description="Latency of LLM API calls from request to first byte of response.",
    unit="s",
    explicit_bucket_boundaries=[0.1, 0.5, 1.0, 2.0, 5.0, 10.0, 30.0],
)

# ai.agent.cost.usd.counter
cost_counter = meter.create_counter(
    name="ai.agent.cost.usd",
    description="Estimated cost of LLM API calls in USD.",
    unit="{usd}",
)

def record_llm_call(
    duration_s: float,
    input_tokens: int,
    output_tokens: int,
    model: str,
    environment: str = "production",
    gen_ai_system: str = "openai",
    error_type: str | None = None,
) -> None:
    """Record metrics for a single LLM call using the standard tag set."""
    # Required tags always present
    required_tags = {
        "gen_ai.system": gen_ai_system,
        "gen_ai.request.model": model,
        "environment": environment,
    }
    # Optional tag: only include if the value comes from a controlled enum
    if error_type is not None:
        required_tags["error_type"] = error_type

    llm_duration_histogram.record(duration_s, required_tags)
    input_tokens_counter.add(input_tokens, required_tags)
    # Estimate cost (illustrative — use actual pricing tables)
    cost_usd = (input_tokens / 1_000_000) * 2.50 + (output_tokens / 1_000_000) * 10.00
    cost_counter.add(cost_usd, required_tags)

The following Python script reads a metrics manifest (a YAML file listing every metric the service emits) and enforces naming convention and cardinality rules. It is run as a CI step on every pull request:

# metrics/manifest.yaml
# One entry per metric family emitted by this service.
metrics:
  - name: ai.agent.llm.request.duration_seconds.histogram
    description: "Latency of LLM API calls in seconds."
    tags:
      - gen_ai.system     # required; enum: openai, anthropic, google_vertex_ai
      - gen_ai.request.model  # required; from model registry
      - environment       # required; enum: production, staging, development
      - error_type        # optional; enum: rate_limit, timeout, content_filter, auth_error
    cardinality_budget:
      gen_ai.request.model: 20
      error_type: 10

  - name: ai.agent.llm.input_tokens.counter
    description: "Cumulative input/prompt tokens sent to LLM providers."
    tags:
      - gen_ai.system
      - gen_ai.request.model
      - environment
    cardinality_budget:
      gen_ai.request.model: 20

  - name: ai.agent.tool.call.duration_seconds.histogram
    description: "Latency of tool executions invoked by the agent."
    tags:
      - tool_name         # optional; from registered tool catalog
      - environment
      - error_type
    cardinality_budget:
      tool_name: 50
      error_type: 10

  - name: ai.agent.cost.usd.counter
    description: "Estimated USD cost of LLM API calls."
    tags:
      - gen_ai.system
      - gen_ai.request.model
      - environment
    cardinality_budget:
      gen_ai.request.model: 20

A mid-size company runs three agentic AI products: a customer support bot, a research assistant, and an internal code review agent. Before adopting naming standards, each team had their own metric namespace. The support team used
, and the code review team used
. A platform-level cost dashboard was impossible to build because none of the metrics aggregated.
After adopting the standard, all three teams publish
tags. A single Grafana panel with a

query shows every team's LLM latency broken down by model — giving the platform team a complete cost attribution view and allowing each product team to see their own data in the same dashboard without any extra configuration.
Mixing _ms and _seconds across teams means a Grafana panel that plots both shows one line at 1,200 and another at 1.2 — they represent the same latency but differ by 1,000x. Standardise on seconds.
Embedding version numbers in metric names.
Names like agent_tool_calls_total_count_v2 encode version into the name instead of using tags. This breaks all historical queries when v3 ships. Use a version tag or omit the version entirely.
Prometheus label names use snake_case. OTel attribute names use dot notation. Mixing these (e.g., myAgent.input_tokens.Counter) creates names that some backends reject and all humans find confusing.

Any tag value that comes from user input (user name, query text, file name) is unbounded and will cause a cardinality explosion. These values belong in trace attributes or log fields, not metric labels.
A metric named ai.agent.llm.latency_seconds could be a gauge, a histogram, or a counter. The aggregation type suffix makes the instrument type self-documenting and prevents misuse in alert rules.
Not declaring cardinality budgets for optional tags.
Adding tool_name as a tag without documenting a budget lets the tag accumulate unlimited values as new tools are added. Always declare the maximum expected cardinality in the schema manifest.

Publish a metrics schema registry — a central Git repository of YAML manifests listing every approved metric, its tags, cardinality budgets, and the team that owns it. This is the authoritative source for CI validation.
Require a schema manifest PR for any new metric or new tag on an existing metric. The review checklist should include: naming compliance, cardinality estimate, unit correctness, and owner assignment.

Run the CI validation script as a required status check so that non-compliant metrics cannot be deployed. The script should also check that the manifest file is in sync with the metrics actually emitted by the service (integration test against a running instance).

Align your metric names with OTel GenAI semantic convention attribute names where possible. If OTel defines gen_ai.usage.input_tokens as a span attribute, name the corresponding metric ai.agent.llm.input_tokens.counter so the connection is obvious.

Use a model registry for the gen_ai.request.model tag values. The registry defines the canonical string for each model (gpt-4o, claude-3-7-sonnet-20250219, etc.) and prevents ad-hoc aliasing (GPT4, gpt4_latest, gpt-4-0613) from splitting cardinality across multiple time series.

Cardinality is the primary scalability constraint for metrics systems. A time series database like Prometheus or Mimir stores one series per unique combination of metric name + all tag values. For an agentic AI system, the series count is approximately:
series = metrics × environments × models × (optional tag combinations)

For example: 20 metrics × 3 environments × 15 models × 50 tools = 45,000 series. This is manageable. But if tool_name is allowed to take any value (e.g., 10,000 distinct tool invocation paths), it becomes 300,000,000 series — far beyond what most time series databases handle efficiently.
Set Prometheus recording rules to pre-aggregate high-cardinality combinations during ingestion, so dashboards query the aggregated data rather than the raw series.
Use OTel Collector's aggregate processor to normalise tag values before they reach the backend — for example, canonicalising all variations of a model name to a single registered value.
Monitor cardinality per metric family with a weekly report (e.g., a Prometheus query counting distinct label combinations). Alert when a metric family exceeds 150% of its declared budget.

OTel Python/JS SDK: the get_meter / create_histogram / create_counter APIs enforce that metric names and unit strings are set at registration time, making it natural to bake the name into a library function.
Prometheus cardinality explorer (tsdb-analyze): audits an existing TSDB to find which metric families and label combinations consume the most series — useful for retroactive cleanup.
Grafana Mimir and Cortex: both support tenant-level cardinality limits and will reject writes that exceed the configured series limit per tenant, acting as a hard enforcement backstop.
conftest (OPA/Rego): can enforce metric manifest rules in CI using a Rego policy file, providing language-agnostic validation without a bespoke Python script.
DataDog Metrics without Limits: allows defining a tag subset to index at full cardinality while aggregating the rest, useful for managing cost when full tag fidelity is not needed for all queries.
Org-wide naming standard with CI enforcement
OTel GenAI conventions only (no org standard)
Medium — LLM calls consistent, orchestration varies

Metrics backends are often less access-controlled than primary databases. A tag like email or user_id leaks PII to anyone with dashboard access. The Forbidden tag tier in your standard prevents this class of mistake.

Exposing gen_ai.request.model in dashboards without access control reveals which models the organisation uses and their relative usage volumes — potentially sensitive competitive information. Ensure metric dashboards respect the same access control as the underlying systems.

A metric rename is a breaking change for all alerts and dashboards that reference the old name. The naming standard — and the governed deprecation process it implies — prevents accidental renames from triggering false "metric absent" alerts.

for your observability data. Just as you would not change a REST API endpoint path without a versioning strategy, you should not change a metric name without a deprecation plan. The naming standard is the schema, and the cardinality budget is the rate limit — both must be agreed upon before a metric goes to production.

Tags represent the dimensions along which you want to slice the data. The key question for each proposed tag is: "will this value come from a finite, enumerable set that I control?" If yes, it may be a valid tag. If no — if the value could be any string a user types, or any UUID generated at runtime — it belongs in a trace attribute or log field, not a metric label.
The {namespace}.{subsystem}.{metric_name}.{unit_suffix}.{aggregation_type} pattern makes every metric self-documenting and enables cross-team querying with a single PromQL or OTel Metrics query.
Always use seconds as the time unit suffix — never milliseconds. Standardise dimensionless counts with _total and dollar costs with _usd.

Classify every tag as required (low-cardinality enum), optional (controlled cardinality with a declared budget), or forbidden (unbounded free text that belongs in traces). Never allow forbidden values.
Enforce naming and cardinality rules with a CI validation script that reads a per-service metrics manifest. Manual review alone does not scale.
The gen_ai.request.model tag value must come from a model registry — not ad-hoc strings — to prevent cardinality fragmentation across model versions and aliases.
Treat metric names as public API contracts: changes require a versioning strategy, a deprecation timeline, and owner approval — exactly like a REST endpoint rename.
